questions.question: What is a design token in the context of a CSS design system?
options: An atomic design value (color, spacing, typography) stored as a CSS custom property
A CSS class used to style components
A token used for CSS authentication
A naming convention for CSS files
questions.explanation: Design tokens are the atomic values of a design system — colors, spacing, typography, shadows — stored as CSS custom properties for consistency.

questions.question: What is the difference between a design system and a component library?
options: A design system includes guidelines and process; a component library is just the code
They are the same thing
A design system is only for designers; a component library is for developers
A component library is always larger than a design system
questions.explanation: A design system includes the code (component library) PLUS guidelines, principles, documentation, governance, and processes.

questions.question: Which methodology is BEST for component naming in a design system?
options: BEM (.c-btn--primary)
OOCSS only
Inline styles
Element selectors like 'button'
questions.explanation: BEM provides clear, consistent naming for components (.c-btn for block, .c-btn--primary for modifier) with flat specificity.

questions.question: How do you implement dark mode in a design system?
options: Override token values in [data-theme='dark'] selector
Duplicate all component CSS files
Use JavaScript to swap all colors
Use CSS filter: invert() on the whole page
questions.explanation: Override only the tokens that change under [data-theme='dark']. Components reference tokens, so they automatically update.

questions.question: What naming convention should design tokens follow?
options: --category-property-variant (e.g., --color-primary-hover)
camelCase (e.g., colorPrimaryHover)
snake_case (e.g., color_primary_hover)
Any convention is fine as long as it's consistent
questions.explanation: --category-property-variant is the most common and readable convention, matching CSS custom property naming patterns.

questions.question: What is a common base unit for spacing scales in design systems?
options: 4px
3px
5px
10px
questions.explanation: 4px is the most common base spacing unit because it divides evenly into 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48 — creating a harmonious scale.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the @layer at-rule in a design system?
options: To control cascade order between tokens, components, and utilities
To split CSS into separate files for performance
To create visual layers in the browser
To organize JavaScript imports
questions.explanation: @layer lets you define explicit cascade order — tokens in the lowest layer, utilities in the highest — preventing specificity wars.

questions.question: Which prefix is commonly used for design system classes?
options: .c- for components, .o- for objects, .u- for utilities
.ds- for everything
.comp- for all classes
.sys- for all classes
questions.explanation: Common prefix conventions: .c- (components), .o- (objects/layout), .u- (utilities), .is- (states).

questions.question: What should every interactive component in a design system include?
options: A visible :focus-visible style for keyboard users
A hover animation
A JavaScript click handler
An icon
questions.explanation: Every interactive component must have a visible focus indicator for keyboard accessibility. :focus-visible is the modern standard.

questions.question: Why should design system components never hard-code values like 'background: #3b82f6'?
options: Hard-coded values break theming — changes require editing every component file
Hard-coded values are slower to parse
Hard-coded values are invalid CSS
Hard-coded values cannot be overridden
questions.explanation: If components hard-code values, you can't theme the system by changing tokens. Components must reference var(--color-primary).

questions.question: What does a modular typography scale accomplish?
options: Creates harmonious, proportional font sizes using a consistent ratio
Makes all text the same size
Creates responsive typography automatically
Replaces the need for font-face declarations
questions.explanation: A modular scale (e.g., 1.25 Major Third) creates proportional font sizes where each level relates mathematically to the next.

questions.question: What is a key responsibility when versioning a design system?
options: Changing a design token value is a breaking change (major version bump)
Versioning is optional for CSS-only design systems
Only component additions need version bumps
Documentation changes require version bumps
questions.explanation: Token changes affect ALL components, making them breaking changes. Use semantic versioning: token changes = major, new components = minor.

questions.question: Which CSS feature allows components to respond to their container size rather than viewport?
options: Container queries (@container)
Media queries (@media)
Flexbox
CSS Grid
questions.explanation: @container queries let components adapt based on their container's inline size — essential for reusable design system components.

questions.question: What is the minimum WCAG AA contrast ratio for normal text?
questions.explanation: WCAG AA requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18px bold or 24px regular).

questions.question: What does a 'layout primitive' in a design system provide?
options: Reusable layout patterns like grids, stacks, and containers (class-based)
Primitive HTML elements like div and span
CSS reset and normalize styles
JavaScript utility functions
questions.explanation: Layout primitives (like .o-grid, .o-stack, .o-container) are reusable CSS classes for common layout patterns.

questions.question: How should design system components handle reduced motion preferences?
options: Wrap animations in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)
Remove all animations — users don't need them
Use CSS will-change to speed up animations
Add longer animation durations instead
questions.explanation: Use prefers-reduced-motion to disable or simplify animations for users who prefer reduced motion.

questions.question: What is the purpose of utility classes like .u-mt-md in a design system?
options: Single-purpose overrides for rapid one-off adjustments
Component class for spacing styles
JavaScript utility functions for CSS
Utility classes for meta-programming
questions.explanation: Utility classes provide quick, single-purpose overrides (margin-top, text-align, display) for one-off scenarios without writing custom CSS.

questions.question: What is a 'living style guide' or 'design system documentation page'?
options: An interactive page showing every component with all variants, states, and usage code
A PDF document listing design tokens
A Figma file with component designs
A README file in the project repository
questions.explanation: A living style guide is an interactive web page where developers can view, test, and copy code for every component in the design system.

questions.question: What should you do when you need a variant of a component not in the design system?
options: Request the new variant through the design system governance process
Create a one-off override in your project
Modify the design system source directly
Use inline styles
questions.explanation: Follow the design system's contribution process. Adding variants to the system ensures consistency, documentation, and maintenance.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between CSS custom properties and design tokens?
options: CSS custom properties ARE the implementation of design tokens in CSS
Custom properties replace the need for design tokens
Design tokens replace the need for custom properties
They are unrelated concepts
questions.explanation: Design tokens are the concept (the named design values). CSS custom properties (--color-primary) are how you implement tokens in CSS.
